fact: Custer rode into the valley with approximately 260 men in his immediate column
source: Son of the Morning Star, Evan S. Connell (1984); estimates of Custer's battalion range from roughly 210–262 depending on how detachments are counted

fact: Custer split the 7th Cavalry regiment into three (or four) separate columns before the attack
source: Battle of the Little Bighorn — National Park Service

fact: The village stretched approximately three miles along the Little Bighorn River and was the largest known gathering of Lakota and Cheyenne
source: Little Bighorn Battlefield National Monument — National Park Service; estimates place the village at 2–3 miles long with 6,000–7,000+ inhabitants

fact: All approximately 210 men with Custer on the ridge were killed, with the fight lasting under an hour by most Native accounts
source: Lakota and Cheyenne oral histories compiled in 'Indian Views of the Custer Fight,' Richard G. Hardorff (2005); widely documented estimate of 210 killed with Custer's immediate command

fact: Soldiers' Springfield Model 1873 carbines were prone to jamming — spent cartridge casings expanded and stuck in the breech
source: Archaeological study of the battlefield by Douglas D. Scott and Richard A. Fox, 'Archaeological Insights into the Custer Battle' (1987); extraction failure of copper cartridges in the Springfield 1873 is well documented

fact: The battle took place on June 25–26, 1876
source: Battle of the Little Bighorn — Encyclopaedia Britannica
